**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may find you in sorrow. Five years have passed since our last correspondence, and the world has turned upon its axis, yet I have not found the strength to write to you, nor the courage to call. The time is heavy upon me, and the sea has grown restless of late.

I write to you now, not for the storm that approaches, for I fear it may be the last I shall see. The winds do howl, the waves do roar, and the night do stretch like a shroud over the land. I have no doubt that I shall not see the dawn.

Yet, I write to you with a heart full of both sorrow and hope, for though the world be dark, I still hold fast to the light. The lighthouse do stand, and I do remain, though I do tremble.

I have longed for your voice, your touch, your presence, and yet I have not dared to seek you. The years have been long, and the silence between us has been a wound. Yet, I have found solace in the light of the beacon, and in the memory of our days past.

I pray that this letter may find you with a smile, though I know it may find you with a tear. For I write to you with a heart full of love, though I fear I may not live to see your answer.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a beast at the door, the sea roiling as if it too has heard the news of our estrangement. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and I've been alone in this tower, save for the sea and the stars and the occasional storm that comes to test me. But tonight—tonight is different. Tonight, the sky is black, the wind is wild, and I fear that I may not see dawn.

I've been thinking of you a lot lately. Not because I miss you, but because I wonder if you still remember me. If you ever did. I've written to you, Eleanor, I've sent letters, I've tried to reach through the silence, but you never answered. And I don't blame you. You were young then, and I was a fool, too proud, too stubborn. I thought I could protect you from the world, but I only locked you out of it.

I used to think that the sea was the only thing that could break me. But I was wrong. It wasn't the sea. It was you. The silence, the absence, the way the world went on without you. I thought I could carry it, but I couldn't. I let the storm come, and I let the rain fall, and I let the wind howl, and I let the night fall in on me,

**To My Dearest, Dearest Daughter,**  
**My Little Light,**  

I sit here, with the wind howling like a beast unleashed, the sea roaring with a fury that makes my bones tremble. The lighthouse is a shadow against the storm, and I am its keeper, its last flicker of fire, its last breath of hope. I have not spoken to thee in five years, and yet tonight, I write this letter not for the past, but for the present—because if I do not, I may not live to see the dawn.  

I have not seen thee in years, and yet I still see thee in every wave, in every whisper of the wind, in every crack of the ice that creeps up the shore. Thou art my light, my only light, and I have let it fade. I have let thee go, like a ship set adrift in a sea that does not know its shore. I have let thee go, and yet I still feel thee in my blood, in my breath, in my soul.
